facts_disclosed: Works under Chef Antoine Dubois at Maison Laurent -- he's been her mentor for 3 years

turns.content: ok so I just got off service and I need to vent before I explode. is anyone even on this thing at 1am lol
I'm here! 1am vent sessions are welcome. What happened during service?

so tonight was an absolute disaster. we had a full dining room, 85 covers, and my saucier called in sick twenty minutes before service. twenty. minutes. I had to run his station AND mine and every time I turned around something was burning or someone was plating wrong
That's a nightmare scenario -- running two stations on a full house is borderline impossible. How did you manage the rest of service? Did anyone step in to help cover?

I mean I survived, barely. the thing is chef Antoine saw everything. he didn't say a word during service, just watched me run back and forth like a lunatic, and then after close he pulls me aside and goes "your jus broke twice on table 12 and 14" and I wanted to scream because YES I KNOW ANTOINE I WAS DOING TWO JOBS

That must have been so frustrating -- you were holding the entire line together and the feedback focused on the small cracks instead of the fact that you kept it from falling apart completely. Is he usually that direct, or was tonight especially harsh?

non non that's the thing, chef Antoine Dubois is ALWAYS like that. the man does not care about your excuses. three years I've been working under him at Maison Laurent and he has never once said "oh well you were short-staffed, c'est bon, we'll let it slide." never. every plate is a lesson. every single one. he told me once that mediocrity doesn't get a pass because the circumstances were difficult

Three years under someone that demanding -- that's serious. It sounds like he holds you to the same standard regardless of what's going wrong around you, which is brutal in the moment but clearly comes from a place of pushing you to be better. Do you feel like his approach has actually made you a stronger chef, even when it's maddening?

putain, yes. that's the part I hate admitting. everything I can do in this kitchen, the speed, the precision, the way I can taste a sauce and know it needs a quarter teaspoon more acid -- that's all him. before I got to Maison Laurent I was decent. now I'm actually good. he broke me down and rebuilt me and I hate him for it and I'm grateful for it at the same time

That kind of mentorship is rare and incredibly valuable, even when it feels punishing. The fact that you can identify exactly what he's given you -- that precision, that palate development -- says a lot about how seriously you take the craft. A lot of people would have walked away from someone that intense after a few months, let alone three years. What keeps you there?

honestly? because I know what I'll be when he's done with me. I'll be the kind of chef who can run any kitchen anywhere in the world. he's classical French, proper Escoffier lineage, trained in Lyon, the whole thing. that kind of knowledge doesn't exist everywhere. and Maison Laurent is his stage, his masterpiece. being sous chef in that kitchen means something

That's a really clear-eyed way to look at it. You're not just enduring it -- you're investing in yourself by staying. A chef with that classical French pedigree passing it down directly to you is the kind of training you can't get from a textbook or a YouTube video. Do you see yourself staying at Maison Laurent long-term, or is the goal to eventually take everything he's taught you and build something of your own?

I don't know yet. right now I just want to be the best sous chef this restaurant has ever had. tonight was not that lol. but tomorrow I'm going in early to prep extra jus because if Antoine catches another broken sauce I will actually die. merde. I should go to sleep it's almost 2am and I'm still wired from the adrenaline

Going in early to prep extra jus is exactly the kind of response that separates good cooks from great ones -- you're not making excuses, you're solving the problem. That said, you also need sleep to perform. Try to wind down a bit before you crash. Even a bad night of sleep is better than no sleep before a long service. Good luck tomorrow -- and for what it's worth, keeping an 85-cover service running on two stations without anyone noticing from the dining room side is pretty impressive, broken jus or not.

hah the dining room doesn't notice because we would rather die than let them notice. that's the kitchen code. ok I'm going, bonne nuit. thanks for letting me vent, I needed that before I started texting Antoine angry paragraphs at 2am which would definitely get me fired

Ha! Yeah, save the angry paragraphs for after you've slept. The 2am version of any text is always more dramatic than it needs to be. Bonne nuit -- go rest, crush that prep tomorrow, and show Antoine what you can do with a full station and proper jus. You've got this.
